comments:
This paper presents iTALS, an ALS-based tensor factorization method adapted for implicit-feedback, context-aware recommendation. The main technical contribution is a generalization of the iALS "implicit trick" to tensor factorizations that yields an algorithm whose per-epoch cost is linear in the number of non-zero observations and cubic in the latent dimension K. Two practical context variants are proposed and evaluated: seasonality (time bands) and sequentiality (using the category of the last purchases). The authors provide a complexity analysis and a short proof (lemma) supporting the computational approach, and evaluate on two proprietary implicit datasets and an implicitized Netflix split, reporting substantial improvements (e.g. large relative gains in recall@20) over baseline iALS.

Strengths:
- Addresses an important and practical problem: scalable context-aware recommendation for implicit data.
- Clear algorithmic idea: sensible generalization of iALS to tensors, with a complexity analysis and a correctness lemma.
- Two useful and interpretable context instantiations (time bands and recent-category) that integrate easily into standard factorization frameworks.
- Empirical evaluation on large real-world datasets (including Netflix implicit variant) showing notable gains.

Weaknesses / Issues and suggestions:
- Comparisons: the experimental section appears to compare mainly to iALS. The paper cites other context-aware methods (e.g. multiverse/tensor methods, factorization machines) but does not show head-to-head empirical comparisons. Adding comparisons with other strong baselines (e.g. Rendle's factorization machines, Multiverse tensor method, context-aware matrix factorization variants) would strengthen the evaluation and help quantify the advantage of the proposed formulation.
- Reproducibility and data details: two of the three datasets are proprietary. More details on dataset statistics (users, sparsity, distribution of events per user/item), exact train/test splits, and hyperparameter choices would improve reproducibility and interpretability. Release of code and (preprocessed) data or at least the hyperparameters used would be valuable.
- Weighting scheme: the algorithm depends on the weight tensor W (weights >1 for observed entries). The paper mentions several ways to compute weights but does not describe precisely which scheme was used in experiments or study sensitivity to the weighting design. An ablation on weighting strategies would be useful.
- Runtime and scalability numbers: the complexity analysis is good, but reporting concrete runtimes, memory usage, and scaling with K and number of contexts would help readers assess practical feasibility.
- Baselines and ablations: include ablation studies showing the contribution of each component (e.g. context features vs. no context, number of context dimensions, effect of K, epochs). Also consider comparing to simple pre/post-filtering context methods to quantify the value of modeling context jointly.
- Presentation: the provided text contains numerous typographical/spacing artifacts (likely OCR). While the technical content is understandable, polishing the manuscript will help clarity.

Overall assessment: this is a solid, practically relevant contribution that extends ALS-style implicit factorization to tensors in a scalable way and demonstrates useful context applications. With improved empirical comparisons, more details on weighting and runtime, and minor polishing, the paper would make a strong contribution to the recommender systems literature.
